summary: Gruber examines a Quarkslab white paper claiming Apple could read iMessages despite its end-to-end encryption promises. He draws a careful distinction between 'can' and 'could be,' arguing that what researchers proved is a theoretical vulnerability in Apple's key infrastructure, not that Apple is actually intercepting messages. He criticizes Ars Technica for overstating the findings. Gruber argues that Apple's corporate credibility would be at enormous risk if they were lying, making deception an irrational business decision. He also notes Apple's design philosophy of storing as little user data as possible, whether motivated by genuine privacy concern or legal liability avoidance.
key_points: Quarkslab proved Apple's key infrastructure could theoretically allow interception, not that Apple is actually reading messages — a critical semantic distinction
Apple's public denial would represent an enormous corporate credibility risk if proven false, especially in the post-Snowden era
The PR calculus doesn't favor lying: most iMessage users don't even know about end-to-end encryption, so the gain is small while the risk of exposure is severe
Apple's internal design philosophy reportedly prioritizes storing nothing on servers so there's nothing for law enforcement to request
Ultimately, users must take Apple's word for it since there's no way to cryptographically verify iMessage's server-side implementation
money_quotes: It comes down to Apple's word.
That's not what Quarkslab proved. What they proved is that Apple could be, and that we as users have no way to verify cryptographically that they are not.
This is in many ways a semantic argument over the difference between can and could be.
If you believe or even suspect that Apple is lying about this, consider at least that Apple is taking an enormous risk by doing so.
To lie would be to take an enormous PR risk for a relatively small PR gain.
Apple seeks to wash its hands of any possible involvement in such matters.
Storing nothing at all is the only way to protect it.
key_insight: The iMessage encryption debate is really about whether Apple deserves trust, since the system's design makes independent verification impossible — but the corporate risk calculus strongly favors Apple telling the truth.
